def.zoomer: OK Boomer is the boomer phrase now. The irony is complete.

archive.name: Hot girl summer

def.normie: Coined by Megan Thee Stallion in 2019 — a summer of confident, unapologetic, female enjoyment. Became a permanent template (white boy summer, tomato girl summer, etc.).
def.normal: The grandparent meme of all '[X] summer' formats. Megan made it permanent vocab.
def.zoomer: Hot girl summer is just an annual event now. Permanent.

archive.vid: hot girl summer megan thee stallion

def.normie: A September 2019 Facebook event jokingly proposing to raid Area 51 in Nevada to 'see them aliens.' Two million RSVPs. About 150 people actually showed up.
def.normal: Last truly universal pre-pandemic meme. The Naruto-run aesthetic. Generated music festivals and merch.
def.zoomer: Storm Area 51 was the cinematic universe. They can't stop all of us.

archive.name: Distracted Boyfriend

def.normie: A 2017 stock photo of a man on a sidewalk looking back at a passing woman while his girlfriend looks shocked. Became the canonical 'desire vs. commitment' meme template.
def.normal: One of the most reused stock-photo templates of the late 2010s. Still occasionally used; aging gracefully.
def.zoomer: Distracted boyfriend is canon meme history. Use it once a year max.

archive.origin: Shutterstock photo by Antonio Guillem, 2015 → meme 2017

def.normie: A series of stock photos of Hungarian retiree András Arató, smiling in a forced way that suggests hidden suffering. The original 'masked pain' template.
def.normal: Harold's been a meme since 2014. The 'I'm fine' format is timeless because the emotion is universal.
def.zoomer: Harold is permanent. Harold knows.

def.normie: Turkish chef Nusret Gökçe sprinkling salt over meat with a theatrical wrist-flick. The 2017 video and pose became a universal 'finishing touch' template.
def.normal: Salt Bae built a steakhouse empire on the meme. The pose is permanent; the actual restaurant pricing is its own meme.
def.zoomer: Salt Bae is dad-zone now. Used unironically by your uncle.

archive.name: Mannequin Challenge

def.normie: A November 2016 challenge where groups froze in place while a camera moved through them, often set to Rae Sremmurd's 'Black Beatles.' Schools, sports teams, the Clinton campaign all participated.
def.normal: Last universal pre-Trump-era challenge. Six weeks of total cultural dominance, then evaporated.
def.zoomer: Mannequin Challenge was peak pre-doom internet. The freeze-frame format walked so much followed.

archive.origin: Edward H. White High School video, Oct 2016

def.normie: A February 2016 Vine of teenager Daniel Lara wearing white Vans, with friend Joshua Holz repeatedly saying 'damn, Daniel.' Three days of total cultural dominance.
def.normal: Pure Vine-era distillation. The white Vans got Daniel a year of free shoes. Pre-Trump America's last gift.
def.zoomer: Damn Daniel was the Vine era in three seconds. We will never have it again.

def.normie: In late 2017/early 2018, a 'joke' about eating Tide Pod laundry detergent capsules became a panic about teens actually doing it. The actual challenge was rare; the discourse was massive.
def.normal: Manufactured-panic meme that the meme community ran with ironically before mainstream media caught on.
def.zoomer: Tide Pods were the prelude to every 'kids are doing dangerous things' moral panic since.

def.normie: A May 2018 audio illusion where people heard either 'Yanny' or 'Laurel' from the same clip. Internet split into two warring camps for one week.
def.normal: The audio counterpart to The Dress (2015). Three days of viral discourse, then total evaporation.
def.zoomer: Yanny vs Laurel was a moment. Then it was over. Audio illusions don't have legs.

def.normie: A December 2018 meme of an overweight Bugs Bunny from a 1941 cartoon, paired with the name 'Big Chungus.' Briefly dominant on Reddit.
def.normal: Pure r/dankmemes content. Faded fast but became permanent ironic vocabulary for anything chunky.
def.zoomer: Chungus is still in the vocabulary. The bunny is permanent.

archive.name: Is this a pigeon?

def.normie: A 1991 anime screenshot of a character labeling a butterfly as a pigeon, used as a 'misidentification' template since 2018.
def.normal: One of the most enduring exploitable image templates of the late 2010s. Still occasionally relevant.
def.zoomer: Is this a pigeon? is one of the safe-to-use vintage templates.

archive.origin: The Brave Fighter of Sun Fighbird (1991) → meme 2018

def.normie: A 2015 image macro from Drake's 'Hotline Bling' music video — Drake rejecting one option, approving another. The canonical 'preference' template.
def.normal: Permanent vocab. The Drake-no/Drake-yes format is still in heavy use as a brain-dead-tier comparison meme.
def.zoomer: Drake yes/no is meme infrastructure now. Used by your boss in slide decks.

def.normie: A 2013 meme featuring a Shiba Inu with broken-English captions in Comic Sans ('such wow', 'very meme', 'much doge'). Spawned Dogecoin and Elon Musk discourse for a decade.
def.normal: The og Shiba meme. Spawned a cryptocurrency that became a billion-dollar joke. Permanent meme infrastructure.
def.zoomer: Doge is sacred. The original Kabosu passed in 2024 and the internet briefly mourned.

archive.name: Harlem Shake

def.normie: A February 2013 video challenge set to Baauer's 'Harlem Shake' — one person dancing alone, hard cut to entire room in costume going wild. Tens of thousands of videos in two weeks.
def.normal: The 30-second video format that broke YouTube. Brief, intense, perfectly archived.
def.zoomer: Harlem Shake was YouTube 2013 in a single format. Iconic. Done.

archive.origin: Filthy Frank → Sunny Coast Skate YouTube, Feb 2013

def.normie: An August 2014 challenge to dump a bucket of ice water on your head for ALS awareness (and donate, ideally). Raised $115 million for the ALS Association in eight weeks.
def.normal: The challenge format that proved viral can do tangible good. Replicated annually for various causes but never recaptured.
def.zoomer: Ice Bucket Challenge is the rare 'viral did something' case. Still cited as the template.

def.normie: In January 2025, facing a US TikTok ban, American users mass-migrated to Chinese app Xiaohongshu (RedNote), causing a brief Sino-American cultural exchange moment.
def.normal: The 'TikTok refugees' arc was a Q1 2025 phenomenon. Lasted as long as the actual ban situation did.
def.zoomer: RedNote was the funniest thing about the TikTok ban panic. Still on phones, never opened.

def.normie: A 2022 fashion and aesthetic revival of late-90s/early-00s style — low-rise jeans, butterfly clips, flip phones, frosted lip gloss.
def.normal: Driven by Gen Z TikTok and concurrent indie sleaze revival. Mostly survives in fashion.
def.zoomer: Y2K is just regular fashion now. Promoted from trend to baseline.

def.normie: A 2021-22 revival of the messy, flash-photography, party-photo aesthetic of late-2000s hipster Brooklyn (Cobrasnake era).
def.normal: Pre-recession aesthetic nostalgia. Influenced fashion (Adidas Sambas), party culture, and concurrent Y2K vibes.
def.zoomer: Indie sleaze was a brief 2022 moment. Sambas survived.

def.normie: Discourse around 'traditional wife' content creators (Nara Smith, Hannah Neeleman aka Ballerina Farm) presenting an idealized 1950s-style homemaker life.
def.normal: Ongoing culture-war flashpoint — is it aspirational, problematic, or pure influencer aesthetic? Discourse rotates quarterly.
def.zoomer: Tradwife discourse never dies, it just rotates. Always semi-current.

def.normie: The 2021-22 explosion of NFTs (non-fungible tokens) — Bored Apes, CryptoPunks, profile-pic art selling for millions. Most projects later collapsed.
def.normal: Beeple's $69M auction kicked it off; the 2022 crash ended it. Now mostly remembered as a cautionary tale.
def.zoomer: NFTs were a moment we'd all like to forget. The apes still exist; nobody talks about them.

def.normie: A 2021-22 movement claiming 'Web3' (blockchain-based decentralized internet) would replace the current internet. Mostly evaporated by 2023.
def.normal: Companion to NFT mania. Spawned endless 'gm' tweeting and Discord servers. Quietly faded as crypto winter arrived.
def.zoomer: Web3 was the cope of 2022. The discourse just stopped one day.

archive.name: Mukbang peak

def.normie: Korean-origin 'eating broadcast' videos where creators consume massive amounts of food on camera. Peaked in popularity 2018-19.
def.normal: Persistent format that survives in niches — ASMR mukbang, challenge mukbang, etc. Mainstream peak is over.
def.zoomer: Mukbang lives in specialized lanes now. The genre persists, the central moment doesn't.

archive.origin: South Korean Afreeca TV, late 2000s → global YouTube 2015-19

def.normie: AJ and his son 'Big Justice' rate Costco food items with the catchphrase 'we bring the BOOM,' rating things on a scale that goes up to 'a BOOM or a doom.' Exploded in late 2024.
def.normal: Father-son Costco rating duo whose 'BOOM' scoring system and 'rate the rizz' bits became inescapable for a season.
def.zoomer: Big Justice rating everything a BOOM was the 2024 dad-content singularity. Iconic, retired.

archive.name: Chill Guy

def.normie: A cartoon dog standing upright in a sweater and jeans with a relaxed half-smile, captioned 'my new character is a chill guy who's actually okay with everything.' Went viral late 2024.
def.normal: The 'chill guy' reaction drawing became shorthand for unbothered detachment before brands and a token scheme ground it down.
def.zoomer: Chill guy was just him. Then everyone wanted a piece and it stopped being chill.

archive.origin: Artist Phillip Banks drawing → meme, Oct 2024

def.normie: A baby pygmy hippo born at Thailand's Khao Kheow Zoo in 2024 became a global internet sensation for being slippery, round, and dramatic. Her name means 'bouncy pork.'
def.normal: The 2024 zoo-animal main character. Spawned makeup tutorials, merch, and an SNL cold-open cameo.
def.zoomer: Moo Deng was the chaotic queen we needed. Still precious, no longer trending.

def.normie: A surreal YouTube animated series (2023) about singing heads emerging from toilets battling camera-headed people. Became the defining 'Gen Alpha brainrot' reference.
def.normal: DaFuq!?Boom!'s absurd series detonated kids' content and gave the world 'skibidi' as an all-purpose nonsense word.
def.zoomer: Skibidi Toilet was the brainrot big bang. The word outlived the series.

def.normie: Pinkfong's 2016 children's song with a do-do-do-do hook exploded globally in 2018-19, became the most-viewed YouTube video ever, and lodged itself permanently in every parent's head.
def.normal: The earworm that conquered the planet and broke YouTube's view record. Inescapable in 2019.
def.zoomer: Baby Shark is a cultural war crime and a billion-view juggernaut. Respect, retired.

def.normie: A 2016 webcomic by KC Green of a cartoon dog sitting in a burning room saying 'This is fine.' Became the universal template for denial amid disaster.
def.normal: From Gunshow comic to permanent reaction format for any 'everything's on fire but I'm coping' moment. Still in heavy rotation.
def.zoomer: This is fine is permanent infrastructure. The dog is all of us, every news cycle.

def.normie: In May 2016, Candace Payne filmed herself joyfully laughing while wearing a Chewbacca mask in a Kohl's parking lot. The Facebook Live video became the most-watched live video at the time.
def.normal: Peak wholesome Facebook-era virality. Pure contagious joy that briefly united the internet.
def.zoomer: Chewbacca Mom was a simpler time's main character. Iconic, archived.

archive.name: The Dress (blue/black or white/gold)

def.normie: In February 2015, a washed-out photo of a dress split the internet over whether it was blue-and-black or white-and-gold. It became a viral debate about color perception.
def.normal: The original 'internet breaks over a perception illusion' event. Spawned think-pieces, science explainers, and endless arguments.
def.zoomer: The Dress walked so Yanny/Laurel could run. Foundational illusion lore.

archive.vid: the dress blue black white gold

archive.name: Grumpy Cat

def.normie: A cat named Tardar Sauce whose facial structure gave her a permanently grumpy frown. A 2012 Reddit photo launched a merchandising empire and the 'No.' caption format.
def.normal: The defining 2010s image-macro cat. Spawned books, a movie, and endless 'I had fun once, it was awful' captions.
def.zoomer: Grumpy Cat is sacred meme royalty. RIP Tardar Sauce (2019).

archive.origin: Reddit photo of Tardar Sauce, Sept 2012

def.normie: A 2011 animation of a pixel-art cat with a Pop-Tart body trailing a rainbow as it flies through space, set to a looping Japanese vocal track. Pure early-2010s absurdity.
def.normal: Peak surreal YouTube-loop era. Later sold as an NFT for ~$590k in 2021, which was its own meme.
def.zoomer: Nyan Cat is foundational internet art. The rainbow trail is eternal.

archive.name: Rickroll

def.normie: A bait-and-switch prank where a disguised link sends you to the music video for Rick Astley's 1987 hit 'Never Gonna Give You Up.' A foundational internet gag since 2007.
def.normal: The original link bait-and-switch. Still deployed today; genuinely immortal as far as memes go.
def.zoomer: Rickroll is permanent. Getting got in 2026 still stings, respectfully.

archive.vid: rickroll never gonna give you up

def.normie: South Korean artist Psy's 2012 single with a galloping 'horse-riding' dance. It became the first YouTube video to reach one billion views and a global phenomenon.
def.normal: The video that broke YouTube's view counter and proved K-pop's global ceiling didn't exist. Inescapable in late 2012.
def.zoomer: Gangnam Style was the original global YouTube monoculture event. Historic, retired.
